questions.question: Explain the concept of 'virtual functions are hierarchical' with a suitable example.
keyPoints: Virtual function resolution depends on the actual object type (most derived class)
Not on the pointer or reference type used to call it
Example: Base* ptr = new Derived(); ptr->display() calls Derived::display(), not Base::display()
This hierarchical behavior enables runtime polymorphism

questions.question: What is a vtable (Virtual Function Table)? Explain its role in virtual function dispatch.
keyPoints: vtable is a lookup table maintained by compiler for each class with virtual functions
Contains pointers to virtual function implementations
Each object has hidden vptr pointing to class vtable
Runtime lookup uses vptr to find correct function based on actual object type

questions.question: Define pure virtual function and abstract class with examples.
keyPoints: Pure virtual function declared with '= 0' has no implementation
Abstract class contains at least one pure virtual function
Abstract class cannot be instantiated, only used as base class
Example: class Shape { public: virtual void draw() = 0; };

questions.question: Why are virtual destructors necessary? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: Virtual destructor ensures proper cleanup of derived class resources
When deleting through base pointer, calls most derived class destructor first
Without virtual: only base destructor runs causing memory leak
Example: virtual ~Base() {} ensures Derived::~Derived() called when deleting Derived* through Base*

questions.question: What is object slicing? How can it be prevented?
keyPoints: Object slicing occurs when derived object assigned to base object by value
All derived class-specific data and behaviors are lost
Virtual function capability is lost after slicing
Prevention: Use pointers or references instead of pass-by-value, or use smart pointers

questions.question: Explain the diamond problem and how virtual inheritance solves it.
keyPoints: Diamond problem: ambiguity when class inherits from two classes sharing common base
Creates two separate base class subobjects causing ambiguity
Virtual inheritance: keyword 'virtual' in intermediate classes
Ensures only one instance of common base class exists in inheritance hierarchy

questions.question: Differentiate between early binding and late binding with examples.
keyPoints: Early binding: function call resolved at compile time based on pointer/reference type
Late binding (dynamic): resolved at runtime using vtable based on object type
Virtual functions use late binding, regular functions use early binding
Example: Base* b = new Derived(); b->func() - early: Base::func(), late: Derived::func()

questions.question: Write a complete C++ program demonstrating virtual functions are hierarchical. Include base class Shape with virtual function area() and derived classes Circle and Rectangle. Show polymorphism using base class pointer.
keyPoints: Define base class Shape with virtual double area() = 0;
Define Circle class with radius and overridden area() returning π*r*r
Define Rectangle class with length, breadth and overridden area() returning l*b
In main: create derived objects, use base pointer to call area()
Demonstrate pointer to derived calls respective derived::area() - hierarchical dispatch
Show actual object type determines which function executes, not pointer type

questions.question: Explain the complete mechanism of virtual function call resolution at runtime with vtable and vptr.
keyPoints: Compiler creates vtable for each class with virtual functions
Each virtual function gets slot in vtable with pointer to implementation
Each object contains hidden vptr pointing to class vtable
When virtual function called: follow vptr to vtable, lookup function, call implementation
Most derived class vtable contains pointers to its overrides
Resolution depends on actual object type, not reference/pointer type - hierarchical nature

questions.question: Design classes to demonstrate the diamond problem and its solution using virtual inheritance.
keyPoints: Create base class Device with virtual function spec()
Create intermediate classes Keyboard and Monitor inheriting virtually from Device
Create derived class Computer inheriting from both Keyboard and Monitor
Without virtual: ambiguous which Device subobject to use
With virtual: single Device subobject shared, no ambiguity
Show proper constructor call flow and unique base instance

questions.question: Explain the Liskov Substitution Principle in context of virtual functions. How does it relate to 'virtual functions are hierarchical'?
keyPoints: LSP: Objects of derived class should be usable wherever base class objects are expected
Virtual functions enable this by ensuring derived classes properly override base behavior
'hierarchical' means function implementation follows inheritance chain to most derived
If derived doesn't override, base implementation used (but still hierarchical)
Proper override maintains contract established by base class virtual function
Violating LSP breaks the polymorphic contract and hierarchical assumptions
